questions.question:
Given two union compatible relations R1(A,B,C,D) and R2(C,D), what is the result of the operation R1 - (R1 - R2)?

A. R2
B. R1 ∩ R2
C. R1 - R2
D. R1 ∩ R2
questions.to_find: The result of the relational algebra expression R1 - (R1 - R2)

understand.plain: We have two relations R1 and R2 that are union-compatible (same schema). We need to simplify the expression R1 - (R1 - R2). Set difference in relational algebra works like set subtraction: A - B gives all tuples in A that are not in B. By applying set identities, R1 - (R1 - R2) simplifies to the intersection of R1 and R2.

keywords.explain: Returns all tuples that are in relation A but not in relation B
Returns all tuples that are in both relation A and relation B
Two relations with the same number of attributes and compatible domains, allowing set operations between them
keywords.example: R1(A,B) and R2(A,B) with same domain types are union-compatible

understand.visual_alt: Two sets R1 and R2 where R1 - (R1 - R2) is the intersection region of R1 and R2

given.aim: Simplify R1 - (R1 - R2) using set algebra identities

terms.connects: The base relation from which we subtract
The relation being intersected with R1

terms.meaning: Tuples in R1 that are not in R2
terms.connects: The inner subtraction that is then subtracted from R1

given.plan: Apply the set identity: A - (A - B) = A ∩ B. This is a standard set theory result. First compute the inner difference A - B (tuples in A not in B), then subtract that from A, which leaves only those tuples of A that were also in B, i.e., A ∩ B.

steps.title: Recall the set identity
steps.apply: This is a standard set algebra identity. Let A = R1 and B = R2. Then R1 - (R1 - R2) = R1 ∩ R2.
steps.note: Proof: A - (A - B) = A ∩ complement(A - B) = A ∩ complement(A ∩ complement(B)) = A ∩ (complement(A) ∪ B) = (A ∩ complement(A)) ∪ (A ∩ B) = empty ∪ (A ∩ B) = A ∩ B

steps.title: Apply to the given expression
steps.apply: Using the identity from step 1: R1 - (R1 - R2) = R1 ∩ R2. The inner expression (R1 - R2) contains all tuples of R1 that are NOT in R2. Subtracting this from R1 leaves only the tuples of R1 that ARE in R2, which is exactly R1 ∩ R2.
steps.note: The answer is R1 ∩ R2, not R2 alone, because R2 might contain tuples not present in R1.

questions.formula_note: Set difference identity: A - (A - B) = A ∩ B, derived from De Morgan's laws on set complement

questions.question:
Consider the following relational database schemes:
  COURSES(Cno, Name)
  PRE_REQ(Cno, Pre_Cno)
  COMPLETED(Student_no, Cno)

COURSES gives the number and name of all the available courses. PRE_REQ gives the information about which courses are pre-requisites for a given course. COMPLETED indicates what courses have been completed by students.

Express the following using relational algebra:
List all the courses for which a student with Student_no = 2310 has completed all the pre-requisites.
questions.to_find: Relational algebra expression for courses whose all pre-requisites have been completed by student 2310

understand.plain: We want to find all courses where every pre-requisite for that course has been completed by student 2310. This is a division-like problem in relational algebra: find courses C such that all (Cno, Pre_Cno) pairs in PRE_REQ where Cno=C have Pre_Cno in COMPLETED for student 2310. We use the concept of relational division or set difference to express this.

keywords.explain: An operation that finds tuples in one relation that are paired with ALL tuples in another relation; used for 'for all' queries
keywords.example: Find students who have taken ALL courses: ENROLLED ÷ COURSES

keywords.explain: A relation mapping each course to its prerequisite courses
keywords.example: Row (CS301, CS201) means CS201 is a prerequisite for CS301

keywords.explain: Filters tuples from a relation based on a condition
keywords.example: sigma_{Student_no=2310}(COMPLETED) returns all courses completed by student 2310

understand.visual_alt: Three tables: COURSES with Cno/Name, PRE_REQ with Cno/Pre_Cno edges showing prerequisite graph, and COMPLETED with Student_no/Cno rows for student 2310

given.aim: Find courses where ALL prerequisites have been completed by student 2310

terms.meaning: Set of Cno values completed by student 2310
terms.example: sigma_{Student_no=2310}(COMPLETED) gives rows like (2310, CS101), (2310, CS201)
terms.connects: This is the set of prerequisite courses that have been satisfied

terms.meaning: Pairs (Cno, Pre_Cno) where Pre_Cno must be completed before taking Cno
terms.example: (CS301, CS201) means student must complete CS201 before CS301
terms.connects: We need to check if all Pre_Cno for a given Cno are in student 2310's COMPLETED list

terms.meaning: To find A where all conditions hold, use: A - (those where some condition fails)
terms.example: Courses with ALL pre-reqs done = all courses with pre-reqs MINUS courses missing at least one pre-req
terms.connects: Drives the relational algebra expression structure

given.plan: Step 1: Get all courses that have at least one pre-requisite. Step 2: Get the set of courses completed by student 2310. Step 3: Find courses that have some pre-requisite NOT completed by student 2310. Step 4: Subtract those from all courses that have pre-requisites.

steps.title: Get courses completed by student 2310
steps.apply: Select all rows from COMPLETED where Student_no = 2310, then project on Cno to get the set of course numbers completed by this student.
steps.note: This gives the set of Pre_Cno values that are satisfied for student 2310

steps.title: Find pre-requisites NOT completed by student 2310
steps.apply: Get all pre-requisite course numbers from PRE_REQ, then subtract the ones completed by student 2310. This gives pre-requisites that student 2310 has NOT yet completed.
steps.note: We need to rename attributes to ensure compatibility for set difference

steps.title: Find courses with at least one unmet pre-requisite
steps.apply: Join PRE_REQ with the set of unmet pre-requisites. This gives all courses (Cno) that have at least one pre-requisite not yet completed by student 2310.
steps.note: A natural join on Pre_Cno links courses to their uncompleted pre-requisites

steps.title: Final result: courses with ALL pre-requisites completed
steps.apply: Start with all courses that have any pre-requisite (Pi_{Cno}(PRE_REQ)), and subtract those that have at least one unmet pre-requisite. The result is courses where ALL pre-requisites have been completed by student 2310.
steps.note: This is equivalent to relational division: PRE_REQ ÷ C2310 (using the student's completed courses as the divisor)

questions.formula_note: Relational division expressed via set difference: 'for all' queries are answered using A - (A - B) pattern or by finding and subtracting tuples with unmet conditions

questions.question:
Consider the join of a relation R with a relation S. If R has m tuples and S has n tuples then the maximum and minimum sizes of the join respectively are:

A. m*n and 0
B. m+n and 0
C. m*n and m+n
D. m+n and m*n
questions.to_find: Maximum and minimum number of tuples in the natural join of R (m tuples) and S (n tuples)

understand.plain: When we join two relations R and S, the result size depends on how many tuples match on the join attributes. In the best case (maximum), every tuple of R matches every tuple of S, giving m*n tuples. In the worst case (minimum), no tuples match at all, giving 0 tuples in the result.

keywords.explain: Combines tuples from two relations that have equal values on their common attributes
keywords.example: R(A,B) |><| S(B,C): matches rows where R.B = S.B

keywords.explain: Occurs when every tuple in R matches every tuple in S (Cartesian product scenario)
keywords.example: If R has 3 rows and S has 4 rows, max join = 12 rows (when all join attribute values match)

class="hi"/><text x="60" y="50" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">r1</text><rect x="10" y="65" width="100" height="30" class="hi"/><text x="60" y="85" text-anchor="middle" class="sub text-anchor="middle" class="lbl no">Min: 0</text><text x="370" y="158" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">(none match)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table R with m rows and table S with n rows. Maximum join produces m*n rows when all join attribute values match. Minimum join produces 0 rows when no join attribute values match.

given.aim: Determine upper and lower bounds on result size of R join S

terms.meaning: Number of tuples in relation R
terms.example: R has 100 employee records, so m = 100
terms.connects: Contributes to the product m*n in the maximum case

terms.meaning: Number of tuples in relation S
terms.example: S has 50 department records, so n = 50
terms.connects: Contributes to the product m*n in the maximum case

terms.meaning: The common attribute(s) on which R and S are joined
terms.example: R(emp_id, dept_id) joined with S(dept_id, dept_name) on dept_id
terms.connects: Determines how many tuples match: if all values match -> m*n tuples; if none match -> 0 tuples

given.plan: Consider extreme cases. Maximum: all m tuples of R match all n tuples of S on the join attribute (e.g., all have the same join key value), giving m*n tuples. Minimum: no tuple in R has a matching join key value in S, giving 0 tuples.

steps.title: Compute maximum join size
steps.apply: The maximum occurs when every tuple in R matches every tuple in S. This happens when all tuples in both relations have the same value for the join attribute. In that case, the join is equivalent to the Cartesian product, producing m * n tuples.
steps.note: This is the Cartesian product upper bound; natural join can never exceed m*n tuples

steps.title: Compute minimum join size
steps.apply: The minimum occurs when no tuple in R has a matching join attribute value in any tuple of S. In this case, the join produces an empty result with 0 tuples.
steps.note: Unlike Cartesian product, natural join can produce 0 tuples if there are no common join attribute values

questions.formula_note: Natural join size bounds: minimum is 0 (no matching tuples), maximum is m*n (all tuples match, equivalent to Cartesian product)

questions.question:
Given the relations:
  employee(name, salary, dept_no)
  department(dept_no, dept_name, address)

Which of the following queries cannot be expressed using the basic relational algebra operations (Pi, sigma, x, -, union)?

A. Department address of every employee
B. Employees whose name is the same as their department name
C. The sum of all employees' salaries
D. All employees of a given department
questions.answer: C. The sum of all employees' salaries
questions.to_find: Which query requires aggregation and cannot be expressed using basic relational algebra

understand.plain: Basic relational algebra operations are: selection (sigma), projection (Pi), Cartesian product (x), set difference (-), union, and derived operations like join and intersection. These operations work on sets of tuples but cannot perform arithmetic aggregation like SUM, COUNT, AVG. The question asks which query requires such aggregation.

keywords.explain: The fundamental set: selection (sigma), projection (Pi), Cartesian product (x), union, and set difference (-); plus derived join, intersection, rename
keywords.example: sigma_{salary>50000}(employee) selects high-salary employees; these are all tuple-level operations

keywords.explain: Operations like SUM, COUNT, AVG, MAX, MIN that compute a single value over a group of tuples; NOT part of basic relational algebra
keywords.example: SUM(salary) over all employees requires aggregation, not expressible with basic operations

keywords.explain: Adds aggregate function operator (script G) to basic RA to support grouping and aggregation
keywords.example: G_{SUM(salary)} (employee) computes total salary using extended RA

understand.visual_alt: Four query options checked against basic relational algebra expressibility. Options A, B, D can be expressed using joins/selections/projections. Option C requires SUM aggregation which is outside basic RA.

given.aim: Identify the query that requires aggregation and cannot be expressed in basic relational algebra

terms.meaning: Relation storing employee name, salary, and department number
terms.connects: Source of salary data needed for the SUM query in option C
Joined with employee to answer options A, B, D

terms.meaning: Basic RA operates tuple-by-tuple; it cannot compute aggregate values across multiple tuples
terms.example: Pi_{salary}(employee) gives all salary values but cannot add them up
terms.connects: This limitation makes option C inexpressible in basic RA

given.plan: Check each option: can it be expressed using only sigma, Pi, join, Cartesian product, union, set difference? Options involving joins, comparisons, or set operations are expressible. Options requiring SUM, COUNT, AVG etc. are NOT expressible.

steps.title: Check Option A: Department address of every employee
steps.apply: Join employee and department on dept_no, then project name and address. This is expressible using natural join and projection - both basic operations.

steps.title: Check Option B: Employees whose name equals their department name
steps.apply: Join employee and department on dept_no to get combined tuples, then select those where employee name equals department name. Expressible using join, selection, and projection.

steps.title: Check Option C: Sum of all employees' salaries
steps.apply: To compute the sum of salaries, we need to scan all tuples and add up the salary values. This requires an aggregation function (SUM) which is NOT part of basic relational algebra. Basic RA can project out the salary column but cannot add all values together into a single result.
steps.note: NOT expressible in basic RA - requires extended RA with aggregate function operator

steps.title: Check Option D: All employees of a given department
steps.apply: Use selection to filter employees by dept_no. This is a basic selection operation, fully expressible in basic RA.

solution.result: Option C (The sum of all employees' salaries) cannot be expressed using basic relational algebra operations

questions.formula_note: Basic relational algebra (selection, projection, join, Cartesian product, union, set difference) cannot express aggregate operations like SUM, COUNT, AVG; extended RA adds the aggregate function operator for such queries
